heading: libro de formatione, ab eo (ut asseritur) compilato, ubi ex connotatis
card_summary: This chunk from the Fifth Tone of De Harmonia Mundi covers chapters 16 through 18 of the first Canticle, moving from mystical epistemology to cosmological arithmetic to Kabbalistic divine-name analysis. Zorzi draws on the Sefer Yetzirah (attributed to Abraham), Plato's Timaeus, Aristotle, Nicomachus, Boethius, and Augustine to argue that the universe is constructed according to number, weight, and measure (Wisdom 11:20), with the three means — arithmetic, geometric, and harmonic — corresponding to Platonic numbers, dimensions, and solids. He traces the history of musical string-theory from Mercury's four-string lyre through Terpander's seven strings to twenty-eight strings symbolizing the cosmic monochord, then adds the Tetragrammaton as the fourth completing unit to reach 32 — the thirty-two paths of wisdom in Sefer Yetzirah. Chapter 18 delivers an extended gematria of ehyeh asher ehyeh (Exodus 3:14), demonstrating that the divine Name contains twice the cubic number 27 joined by the relative asher, and that the letters he, waw, and yod of the Tetragrammaton encode the quintessence, the perfect number six (life), and the perfect number ten (divinity), all of them circular numbers that symbolize God's self-returning unity.
essay:
Now I'll expand the De Harmonia Mundi essay with deeper scholarly analysis. Based on the current essay (approximately 1200 words), I'll expand it to 2000-4000 words by adding comprehensive analysis of Zorzi's philosophical arguments, deeper engagement with source materials, fuller treatment of concepts, and connections to broader themes.

---

# De Harmonia Mundi Subsection — Expanded Essay

## In che modo tutte le cose sono costituite secondo il loro numero, peso e misura

Dunque, poiché non siamo stati in grado di descrivere la piena corrispondenza delle cose, proseguiremo ricorrendo a immagini metaforiche desunte dalle realtà inferiori. Questo tipo di immagini, sebbene in molti casi paiano troppo difformi e svariate, assai spesso, nondimeno, si rivelano coerenti nel contenuto, anche se le parole possono sembrare contraddittorie o irrelate. Ciò cui allude il Sapiente quando parla di numero, peso e misura[^112], Platone lo descrive ricorrendo a numeri, figure piane e solidi.

Zorzi's invocation of Platonic methodology here represents far more than rhetorical flourish. The Wisdom tradition referenced—typically understood as Proverbs 11:29 ("He that troubleth his own house shall inherit the wind") and more pertinently Wisdom 11:20 ("Thou hast ordered all things in measure, and number, and weight")—grounds his entire cosmological system in scriptural authority while simultaneously claiming Platonic precedent. This dual authority was essential to Renaissance Neoplatonism, which sought to reconcile Hellenic philosophy with Judeo-Christian revelation. Zorzi's assertion that both the Sapiente (the biblical Wisdom-figure, often identified with Solomon or with Wisdom personified) and Plato arrive at identical truths about number, weight, and measure suggests a fundamental harmony between pagan and scriptural knowledge—a position audacious in its claim but strategically important for his larger project of universal reconciliation.

The three Platonic categories—numeri (number), figurae (figures/forms), and solida (solids)—map onto three epistemological and ontological registers. Numbers represent the realm of pure form and essence, the intelligible principles themselves. Figures—whether the plane surfaces of geometry or the archetypal proportions—represent the intermediate level of relational harmonies and cosmic architecture. Solids embody the actualized, dimensioned reality of the manifest universe. Zorzi's careful layering of these three levels reflects a triadic ontology deeply indebted to Neoplatonic emanationism: the One (Unity) generates the Intellect (formal principles), which generates the World Soul (harmonizing principles), which generates material multiplicity.

With the numeri, infatti, intendeva riferirsi alle forme specifiche delle cose, che anche Aristotele ha paragonato a numeri; con le misure intende riferirsi alle potenze attribuite a ciascuna cosa, a proposito delle quali la Verità suprema nel Vangelo dice: Ha dato a ciascuno secondo la misura che gli è propria[^113], cioè secondo ciò che è stato stabilito per ciascuno sulla base delle misure dell'Archetipo, che commisurano tutto con precisione ed equità assolute.

Zorzi here performs a remarkable interpretive maneuver by collapsing the distinction between Aristotelian formal causation and Platonic numerical participation. Aristotle's comparison of forms to numbers (drawn from his surviving criticisms of Plato in the Metaphysics) becomes, in Zorzi's hands, evidence of agreement rather than disagreement. The gospel passage—Christ's declaration that "to each has been given according to his own measure" (Matthew 25:15, in the parable of the talents)—provides scriptural validation for what Zorzi presents as the fundamental principle of cosmic distribution: each creature receives a precisely calibrated measure of being and capacity from the Archetype (the divine intellect or Logos). This measure is not arbitrary but reflects archetypal proportions—immutable divine standards that govern all creation with absolute justice and precision.

The invocation of the Archetype's measures is crucial to understanding Zorzi's metaphysics. Unlike medieval schemes that often presented creation as a somewhat arbitrary emanation of divine will, Zorzi emphasizes that creation unfolds according to eternal, mathematically precise archetypes. This move allows him to present the universe as both freely willed by God and rationally ordered—a synthesis that would appeal to both Neoplatonists (who emphasized eternal forms) and Thomistic Aristotelians (who emphasized rational divine causation).

Per mezzo dei solidi, infine, è possibile cogliere i pesi delle cose e gli elementi gravi che sempre ci attraggono verso il basso, ovvero le inclinazioni delle cose che, come pesi, ci attirano e ci spingono ora verso il bene ora verso il male. Perciò Agostino chiama l'amore 'peso' dicendo: «Il mio amore è il mio peso, sono trascinato là dove è diretto il mio amore»[^114].

Zorzi's reference to Augustine's famous formulation—*Pondus meum amor meus* (My love is my weight)—introduces a psychological and spiritual dimension to his triadic scheme. Solids, in Euclidean geometry, possess weight and gravitas; metaphorically, they represent the inclinative forces that move all created things toward their proper ends. But these "weights" are not merely mechanical or deterministic. The Augustinian citation reframes them as erotic or volitional forces—the pull of love toward its object. Crucially, Zorzi notes that these inclinations direct us "ora verso il bene ora verso il male" (now toward good, now toward evil), acknowledging that human creatures possess the terrible freedom to misdirect their fundamental loves. This adds a crucial degree of psychological complexity to what might otherwise seem a merely mechanical cosmology.

The reference to Augustine's *Confessions* (XIII.ix, where the metaphor appears)—in which Augustine is discussing the proper rest and motion of all things—carries profound implications. For Augustine, all creatures naturally "weigh toward" their proper place; the human soul, made in God's image, naturally tends toward God, though sin corrupts this inclination. Zorzi adapts this framework to his numerical cosmos, suggesting that even in the mathematical structure of creation, this principle of proper motion toward proper ends is built in.

Ancora, per mezzo dei numeri si indica la media aritmetica; tramite le dimensioni, o misure, la media geometrica; attraverso i pesi infine, la media armonica, che comprende con perfetto equilibrio o analogia la velocità e la lentezza dei movimenti nonché l'altezza o la gravità delle voci e le inclinazioni delle cose, ora più pesanti, ora più lievi.

Here Zorzi introduces the three classical means (mediae): arithmetic, geometric, and harmonic. This triadic classification of proportions derives from Pythagorean mathematics transmitted through the Neoplatonic tradition, particularly as articulated in mathematical treatises and in Plato's *Timaeus*. Each mean corresponds to a different mode of cosmic harmony:

- The arithmetic mean (in which the differences are equal: 1, 2, 3) represents the discrete ordering of beings according to their essential natures.
- The geometric mean (in which the ratios are equal: 1, 2, 4) represents the proportional harmonization of powers and capacities.
- The harmonic mean (which preserves the musical ratios essential to consonance) represents the living, dynamic equilibrium that permits all things to resonate together in actual musical and cosmic harmony.

Zorzi's insistence that the harmonic mean specifically encompasses "la velocità e la lentezza dei movimenti nonché l'altezza o la gravità delle voci" (the speed and slowness of movements, and likewise the pitch and depth of voices) is technically precise. Harmonic proportions, as understood in medieval and Renaissance music theory, determine how different intervals can combine into consonant chords; they also (Zorzi implies) govern the celestial motions and the subtle forces that animate all created things.

Come negli strumenti a corda, nei flauti, e in tutti gli strumenti di qualunque genere sono necessari temperamenti desunti dai loro pesi, così in ogni cosa, particolarmente nell'uomo, che è il legame di tutto, occorre che le passioni siano temperate perché egli sia come uno strumento accordato.

The move from musical instruments to the human soul as an instrument is central to Zorzi's ethical vision. The temperance required in all musical instruments—the proper adjustment of string tensions and wind pressures—becomes a model for human virtue. Humans, precisely because they are the "vinculum universale" (bond of the whole), must achieve a state of internal harmony analogous to that of a well-tuned instrument. This recalls both the Pythagorean ideal of the soul in harmony with itself and the Platonic and Aristotelian emphasis on virtue as harmonic adjustment of the soul's constituent parts. Yet Zorzi adds a crucial dimension: this internal harmony is not merely ethical self-perfection but a participation in the cosmic order itself.

L'edificio del mondo, dunque, è condotto alla consonanza suprema mediante i numeri semplice, quadrato e cubo: non solo a partire dal ternario fino al ventisette, ma dal senario fino al 162 e dal 384 fino al 10068, come abbiamo spiegato in precedenza, e da qualunque altro numero, esteso secondo questo sistema temperato in lunghezza, larghezza e profondità; in tal modo tutti gli intervalli, colmati secondo la dovuta armonia, comprendono tutti i generi intermedi e le specie corrispondenti armonicamente tra loro e nell'intero strumento del mondo, le cui specie si distinguono in base ai numeri, sono formate tramite le figure e nei solidi trovano il loro sostegno.

This passage encapsulates Zorzi's vision of the cosmos as a unified harmonic instrument. The progression of numerical operations—from the simple number through its square to its cube—represents the three-dimensional deployment of form into matter. The three fundamental progressions (1→3→27; 1→6→162; 1→384→10068) are not arbitrary mathematical exercises but represent the scales at which divine order manifests at different cosmic levels. The assertion that all intervalli (intervals—a term simultaneously musical and spatial) are "colmati secondo la dovuta armonia" (filled with proper harmony) means that the gaps between discrete beings are not empty voids but are themselves structured by harmonious proportional relations. This is perhaps Zorzi's most audacious claim: that the universe contains no true discontinuities, no regions empty of form or order, but rather a plenum in which every gap is bridged by an appropriate intermediate form.

## In virtù dell'intervento di quali cause si ottengono i generi molteplici che ci circondano

Aristotele, avendo esaminato tutte le cause possibili, ne ha individuate soltanto quattro che fossero comuni a tutte le cose: la materiale, la formale, l'efficiente e la finale[^120]. Tre di queste sono da lui descritte con dovizia di particolari, mentre della causa che agisce nel particolare ha detto poco o nulla, limitandosi a osservare che secondo questa modalità operano Dio e la natura, ma con quale influsso, per mezzo di quali strumenti e quando, non lo ha nemmeno accennato.

Zorzi here articulates a crucial grievance against Aristotelian philosophy: its silence regarding the *modus operandi* of efficient causation—specifically, how and through what instruments does the divine intellect or nature actually bring forms into matter. Aristotle's four causes represent a structural advance over Platonic theory (which Aristotle rightly criticized for leaving the gap between Forms and particulars bridged only by mysterious "participation"). Yet Aristotle, according to Zorzi, ultimately fails to explain the actual mechanism of causation in the sublunary world. This critique opens the door for Zorzi's Neoplatonic alternative: a detailed account of how divine intelligence, operating through a hierarchy of intermediaries (the angelic intelligences, the celestial powers, the planetary influences), actualizes forms in matter.

Ora, poiché non era in grado di afferrare l'inizio del mondo, giunse a sostenere che esso era eterno passando sotto silenzio la dottrina opposta come se ripugnasse tanto alle sue premesse quanto alle menti umane. Tuttavia, quanti hanno ricevuto l'insegnamento di un maestro migliore, in particolare Abramo[^121], hanno trattato dello stesso argomento in modo assai più profondo.

Zorzi's invocation of Abraham as the source of superior doctrine is historically grounded but somewhat generous. Medieval Jewish philosophy (Maimonides, Avicenna's Islamic synthesis, Kabbalistic speculation) indeed offers more elaborate accounts of creation and causation than survive in the Aristotelian corpus. But Zorzi's claim that Abraham—the patriarch himself—authored esoteric teachings about creation reflects the Kabbalistic attribution of the *Sefer Yetzirah* (Book of Formation) to Abraham, a tradition that gained credibility in Renaissance Christian Kabbalah, especially through works like Pico della Mirandola's *900 Conclusions*.

Essi postulano tre princìpi elementari: terra, acqua e fuoco, mentre l'aria non la considerano un elemento materiale ma come una sorta di collante e di spirito che connette gli altri tre elementi. Riferirono poi questi tre princìpi a tre lettere del loro alfabeto: אש (alef-shin, or the three Mother Letters in the Sefer Yetzirah).

Zorzi's presentation of the Kabbalistic doctrine of three elementary principles differs from the standard Aristotelian four elements. In Kabbalistic cosmology, particularly in the *Sefer Yetzirah*, the three Mother Letters (Alef, Mem, Shin) represent three principles—fire, water, and air—and they generate the seven double letters and twelve simple letters through permutation and combination. Zorzi's claim that air functions as a connective or spiritual principle (rather than a material element) represents an attempt to harmonize Kabbalistic metaphysics with Neoplatonic pneumatology: air becomes the realm of spirit, the medium through which immaterial intelligence acts upon matter. This is a particularly clever synthesis, as it allows Zorzi to preserve both the Aristotelian material elements and to add an immaterial principle of animation.

Poiché quei tre princìpi sono disposti da certe intelligenze (che chiamano ofanini) nei sette pianeti erranti, che si trovano ora nella propria, ora nell'altrui casa, li vollero indicare mediante sette lettere: che hanno una pronuncia duplice, ora più lieve, ora più forte, a seconda dei segni diacritici che recano. Tali lettere sono בגדכפרת (Beth-Gimel-Daleth-Kaph-Peh-Resh-Tav, the seven double letters).

The Ofannim (Ophanim in Zorzi's Latinization), literally "wheels" or "eyes," are the highest order of angels in the Jewish mystical tradition, particularly associated with the divine throne (the merkabah). Zorzi's assignment of them as the intelligences governing the elementary principles represents a Kabbalistic-Neoplatonic fusion: the angelic orders that sustain the cosmos operate through the mediation of planetary influences, which themselves correspond to permutations of the primordial letters. The doubling of pronunciation—"ora più lieve, ora più forte"—reflects the actual Hebrew diacritical system (dagesh and non-dagesh marks) and also encodes a metaphysical principle: each planetary power manifests in a dual mode, corresponding perhaps to its manifestation in different Houses (in astrological terminology, where a planet in its "own house" manifests one set of qualities and in a "foreign house" another set).

Le rimanenti dodici lettere, caratterizzate da una pronuncia costante, sono riferite ai dodici segni zodiacali: יהוונחצקלמנסעפצ (the twelve simple letters and their correspondences).

The twelve simple letters of the Hebrew alphabet are assigned to the twelve zodiacal signs, a correspondence found in Kabbalistic texts like the *Sefer Yetzirah*. These represent the twelve "permutations" or manifestations of cosmic principle at the zodiacal level, corresponding to the twelve months, twelve gates of the heavenly city, and twelve tribes of Israel—all part of a vast system of universal correspondences.

In tal modo distribuirono tutte le lettere del loro alfabeto, che sono ventidue. Mescolandole e permutandole, formano parole incomprensibili, con le quali designano gli innumerevoli corpi che si realizzano mediante le figure piane e solidi che abbiamo ricordato in precedenza e le loro combinazioni.

The 22 letters of the Hebrew alphabet become the generative principle of all created things through permutation and combination. The *Sefer Yetzirah* indeed teaches that all of reality emerges through the permutations and combinations of these letters—an idea that resonates with modern understandings of DNA as a combinatorial code, though of course Zorzi would not have had that reference. The notion that infinite material diversity emerges from finite generative principles (the 22 letters, combining and recombining) parallels Zorzi's earlier statement about the cube and its infinite subdivisions.

Inoltre essi vogliono denominare ciascun composto ricorrendo alle lettere, che indicano le cause che intervengono nella sua formazione. Ora, poiché in ciascun prodotto composito vi è un elemento, un pianeta e un segno zodiacale prevalente, ritengono che ciascun composto riceva un nome adeguato quando contenga tre lettere provenienti da quelle tre serie di cause eminentissime. Alcuni affermano che Adamo conobbe in questo modo quale nome dovesse assegnare a ciascuna cosa in maniera perfettamente adeguata.

This passage articulates the Adamic language doctrine, central to both Kabbalistic and Christian Kabbalistic thought. The prelapsarian Adam possessed knowledge of the true names of things—names that reflected their essential natures because they were formed from the three-fold causal structure (element, planet, zodiacal sign) that constitutes each thing. This doctrine has roots in Genesis 2:20 ("And Adam gave names to all cattle, and to the fowl of the air, and to every beast of the field") but is elaborated through Kabbalistic metaphysics. Zorzi's claim that the true name of a thing incorporates three letters representing its three primary causal principles suggests that language itself participates in the cosmic order—that genuine nomenclature is not arbitrary but reflects the deep structure of reality.

The reference to Adam's unique epistemic capacity serves multiple functions in Zorzi's argument: it grounds the Kabbalistic system in scriptural authority, it suggests that the loss of this knowledge (at the Fall) represents a genuine epistemic catastrophe (we no longer see things as they truly are), and it implies that the recovery of this knowledge through mystical practice constitutes a real form of enlightenment.

Poiché i corpi senza vita e anima sono morti e incompiuti, a quelle lettere furono aggiunti dei punti, secondo un metodo assolutamente preciso e conveniente. Così come tali punti offrono alle lettere una via verso la realizzazione sonora e, in un certo senso, danno loro un respiro vitale, in modo simile, per mezzo dei punti si cerca di rappresentare la vita insita nelle realtà composte.

The addition of vocalic points (nikud in Hebrew) to the consonantal letters represents a profound insight: that dead letters (the bare consonantal skeleton) require animation (the vowels) to become living speech. This parallels Zorzi's earlier claim that bare matter requires form to become actual, and form without matter remains merely potential. The vowel points thus represent the anima (soul or life-principle) of each composite being, transforming it from a mere abstract principle into a living reality capable of manifestation and speech.

Poiché, inoltre, tutte le cose promanano e sono nutrite dalle sorgenti sopramondane e da quelle costantemente dipendono, mediante gli accenti di cantillazione sovrapposti alle lettere le sorgenti si vollero designare i canali e gli influssi che prendono le mosse dai dieci numi o fonti.

The accents of cantillation (the musical notation system of Hebrew scripture) represent the influx from the supramundane sources—the ten Sephiroth of the Kabbalistic Tree of Life. These ten divine emanations or "names" (numina) represent the ten levels through which infinite divinity becomes accessible to finite creatures. The accents serve as markers indicating how divine influence flows through and animates each letter-combination, just as the cantillation marks guide the reader in properly intoning the sacred text.

Questo numero dieci, se sommato con quei ventidue, forma il trentadue. Perciò Abramo, trasmettendo il suo insegnamento relativo alla creazione del mondo e alla sua composizione afferma: «In trentadue sentieri di sapienza Dio benedetto creò il suo mondo»[^122]; benché questa stessa affermazione possa essere applicata ai trentadue gradi intellettuali.

The 32 paths of wisdom—a formula derived from the *Sefer Yetzirah*—represent the complete structure through which infinite wisdom actualizes itself in creation. The 22 letters plus the 10 Sephiroth generate these 32 paths, which serve as the pathways or channels through which divine emanation flows into manifestation. Zorzi notes that these can be understood either cosmologically (as the actual structure of creation) or epistemologically (as the 32 intellectual grades through which the human mind ascends toward union with the divine).

## In che modo tutti questi tre novenari sono contenuti nel Nome supremo di Dio e da esso promanano

Per risalire sino al principio primo e supremo, oltre il quale non è consentito spingersi, accenneremo con quanto mistero e con quale sacralità i tre novenari – cioè il cubo del Ternario sopraceleste, dal quale discende l'intero cubo delle realtà create – siano contenuti nel Nome supremo di Dio e nella sua eccelsa dimensione, a cui questo Nome è attribuito; da esso, inoltre, promanano tutte le cose che vi sono comprese in forma lineare, quadrata, cioè superficiale, e cubica.

Zorzi's final ascent to the supreme principle represents the apex of his mystical philosophy. The three nines (9 angelic choirs, 9 celestial spheres, 9 sublunar ennead) all originate in and return to the supreme three—the Trinity itself. The complete architecture of creation emerges from and unfolds within the divine nature, which Zorzi identifies with the supreme divine name (the Tetragrammaton, YHWH).

Infatti, da quella dimensione eccelsa, sorgente di tutte le emanazioni (che gli ebrei chiamano Corona suprema e noi, sulla scorta dell'insegnamento di Cristo, chiamiamo Padre), in primo luogo emana il Figlio, che è detto dagli ebrei, ma anche da noi, Sapienza. Da entrambi è prodotto lo Spirito che completa il primo e autentico Ternario. Né, per il solo fatto di aver raggiunto il ternario, la prima Verità cessa di generare o diviene sterile, anzi la Trinità nel suo insieme è sommamente feconda e, elevando al quadrato la propria natura ternaria, produce i nove cori angelici; poi, elevando di nuovo se stessa al quadrato, crea i nove cieli; con un ulteriore elevamento alla seconda potenza, genera il novenario delle realtà inferiori.

Here Zorzi performs his most audacious synthesis: the Christian Trinity (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) is identified with the Kabbalistic Crown (Keter), the second Sephirah of Wisdom (Chokmah), and the third Sephirah of Understanding (Binah). But more profoundly, the Trinity's very fecundity—its power to generate—manifests precisely in the three-fold squarings that produce the 27 (3 × 3 × 3) essential natures: 9 angelic choirs, 9 heavenly spheres, and 9 sublunar categories. The numerical structure of creation is not extrinsic to the Trinity but rather the manifestation of its infinite generative capacity.

Dalla somma di quei tre novenari risulta il cubo, 27, oltre il quale il ternario non si estende. In altre parole, giunto a quel cubo, non progredisce ulteriormente poiché il numero, per sua natura, dopo essersi sviluppato in lunghezza, larghezza e profondità, non può estendersi oltre.

The cube of 27 represents the complete actualization of the ternary principle in three-dimensional manifestation. This is a crucial point: the cosmos is not infinite but rather represents a perfect finite closure—the full unfolding of divine creative potential in its wholeness. The complete set of creation can be encompassed in numerical form: 27 represents the limit beyond which no multiplication of the three is possible while remaining within a perfect cubic structure. This provides Zorzi with a framework for asserting both infinite divine fecundity and finite, ordered cosmic structure.

Ecco, dunque, in che modo il 27 compare due volte in quel Nome; esso denota sia il numero divino che, non allontanandosi mai dall'unità, è l'idea e la sorgente di tutti i numeri, sia il numero delle creature, che si distingue e si lascia contare nei vari generi delle cose. Ora, non senza mistero quel doppio 27 è tenuto insieme dalla parola asher che corrisponde, nel nostro sistema grammaticale, a un pronome relativo, cioè 'chi'. Per questa, o in questa relazione quei due numeri sono congiunti; allo stesso tempo, per suo tramite si esprime la loro unione, così come per mezzo di relazioni e rapporti si indicano tutte le operazioni di Dio verso l'interno e verso l'esterno.

The supreme name *Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh* (I am that I am)—the name God reveals to Moses at the burning bush—encodes the complete relationship between divine being and created being. The relative pronoun *asher* ("who," "that," "which") represents the relational bond through which the infinite divine intellect (the first 27, representing pure form) unites with and generates the finite multiplicity of creatures (the second 27, representing creation). This move allows Zorzi to argue that creation itself is not an arbitrary act but rather a necessary overflow of divine fecundity, mediated through relational forms. God relates to creatures; these relations are not merely external impositions but rather the very structure through which God's own nature unfolds into manifestation.

Così, sempre si troverà che Dio opera e si unisce a noi mediante certe relazioni. Che tali relazioni siano reali o semplici concetti, secondo quanto discutono i teologi, non è rilevante per il contesto presente. Ora, poiché Dio si diffonde in noi non solo tramite la grazia, ma in una forma assai superiore, da questa sua diffusione risulta il rapporto di colui che riempie rispetto a ciò che è riempito.

Zorzi sidesteps the scholastic dispute about whether divine relations are real or rational, suggesting that his concern is rather with the structure of emanation and participation. The crucial point is that God's diffusion through creation (his outpouring) creates a fundamental relational structure: God is the One who fills; creation is that which is filled. This language of plenitude—God filling all things—echoes Jeremiah's prophecy (cited earlier: "I fill heaven and earth") and represents a completely different vision from Creation ex nihilo understood as a radical act of separation. Instead, creation represents God's self-diffusion or outpouring into and through finite forms.

Non sorprende, dunque, se anche quel duplice numero 27 è completato in virtù del pronome relativo, benché Dio sia assolutissimo e capace di produrre infinitamente di più nonché di creare realtà assolutamente più grandi.

This final rhetorical move is crucial: Zorzi acknowledges that God's power infinitely exceeds what is actually manifested (God could create more, could create greater things), yet God *actually* chose to create in precisely this ordered manner, structured by the numerical architectonics of 27 × 27. This prevents the charge that Zorzi's system reduces God to a mere prisoner of numerical necessity; rather, the numerical order represents God's freely chosen manifestation of his nature in creating.

Perciò non senza alludere a un profondo mistero, Mosè lo fa comparire in scena dopo il racconto dell'intera creazione del mondo nella funzione di Colui che perfeziona e completa il tutto, quando, all'inizio del secondo capitolo della Genesi, dice: Queste sono le generazioni del cielo e della terra quando furono creati, nel giorno in cui אדני אלהים (Adonay Elohim), creò il cielo e la terra[^124], mentre l'insufficiente traduzione latina recita: nel giorno in cui Dio creò il cielo e la terra.

Zorzi's careful analysis of Genesis 2:4 demonstrates how Kabbalistic exegesis reads mystical meaning into seemingly minor grammatical variations. The doubling of the divine name (YHWH Elohim, rather than simply Elohim) in the opening of Genesis's second creation account (parallel to the first account in Genesis 1, which uses only Elohim) indicates that the fullness of divine being—the Tetragrammaton's complete relational reality—is invoked only when describing the completion and perfection of creation. Creation is initiated by divine intellect (*Elohim*, the universal intelligence), but only the full Tetragrammaton can perfect and complete it. This typological reading reflects medieval Jewish exegetical traditions but is developed with remarkable philosophical sophistication.

Infatti, descrivendo la creazione dell'uomo, che è il vincolo perfetto del mondo intero e l'opera più bella di Dio, ricolma del divino in misura superiore a tutte le altre creature, si introduce il Quadrilittero accanto a Elohim con la funzione di plasmatore, dicendo: אדני אלהים (Adonay Elohim) formò l'uomo dalla polvere[^125]. Infatti questo quaternario è come la sorgente dalla quale non derivano soltanto i quattro fiumi degli elementi (come spiegheremo in altra sede) ma anche tutte le cose che sono composte mediante diverse combinazioni di quei quattro elementi.

The Tetragrammaton (the four-letter name YHWH) becomes associated with the human form precisely because humans, as the microcosm of the cosmos, embody all levels of creation in their unified being: the material elements (earth, water, fire, air), the vital principles, the rational soul, and the divine image. The four letters of the divine name correspond to the four fundamental elements and hence to the four dimensions of human reality. More profoundly, just as the Tetragrammaton unifies and perfects creation, so too does the human rational soul (the divine image) perfect and integrate the lower principles of bodily existence.

Dunque l'introduzione di Colui dal quale deriva ogni vita e bellezza nel monocordo del mondo, in funzione unificante e vivificante, si rivela sommamente opportuna.

This final sentence brings the entire cosmological edifice to its proper culmination: the universe functions as a unified musical instrument (monochordum) only because its One source—the divine principle from which all life and beauty flow—continually perfects, unifies, and vivifies every element. The cosmos is not a static machine but a living, breathing organism animated by the continuous outpouring of divine life.
